title: On the difficulty of being a witness without becoming a curator
summary: Examine the ethical tension between witnessing someone else's experience and the impulse to organise, interpret, and present it — and argue that this tension is not resolvable but must be held.
topic: The ethics of testimony, documentation, and the line between bearing witness and appropriating experience
prompt: Write a 600–800-word argumentative essay on the ethics of witnessing: the risk that a witness — a journalist, a photographer, a memoirist, an activist — crosses from recording another person's experience to curating it, shaping it for an audience in ways that serve the witness's own purposes rather than the witnessed person's dignity. Use at least two specific examples from different national or professional contexts. Deploy inversion at least once, a cleft sentence, and a 'granted… even so…' concession. Avoid abstract moralising; work with concrete tensions.
hintStarters: Only when the witness begins to make choices about what to include does the act of recording become the act of composing…
What separates the witness from the curator is not intention but the structure of the choices they make…
Granted, all documentation involves selection. Even so,…

hintVocab.meaningVi: sự kiệt sức lòng trắc ẩn
sự thẩm mỹ hóa nỗi đau
phẩm giá của người được chứng kiến
xung lực ghi chép tài liệu
sự đồng ý có thông tin
ngữ pháp của sự đại diện
lời kể của người sống sót
sự vươn quá của sự đồng cảm
sự đồng lõa trong ghi chép tài liệu

sampleText:
Only when the witness begins to make choices about what to include does the act of recording become the act of composing — and it is at this threshold, barely visible to the witness themselves, that the ethics of documentation become genuinely difficult. A witness who does not select is not a witness but a surveillance camera. Selection is unavoidable. Yet selection is also the beginning of curation, and curation is the beginning of the imposition of meaning that the witnessed person did not consent to and may not recognise as their own.

The tension is most acute in photojournalism. Consider the photographs taken during the 1994 Rwandan genocide, many by Western journalists who were among the very few outsiders present. The images that circulated most widely — bodies on roads, skeletal survivors, the geometry of mass atrocity rendered legible for international consumption — were chosen not by the people they depicted but by picture editors in London, Paris, and New York whose primary obligation was to readers not yet moved to political attention. Kevin Carter's famous photograph of the vulture and the child in Sudan — taken in 1993, awarded the Pulitzer Prize — is perhaps the clearest case of the curatorialist logic inside the documentary impulse: the image was composed to produce a response, the child's own history was irrelevant to the response it produced, and the photographer's career — and death, and legacy — absorbed the moral conversation that should, by rights, have been about the child.

It is precisely the moment when the witness begins to care about the quality of their account — when the prose becomes elegant, when the angle is adjusted, when the detail is selected for its emotional resonance rather than its accuracy — that the transition from witnessing to curating occurs. This is not a failure of intention; it is a structural consequence of the fact that documentation requires a medium, and all media have aesthetics. The oral historian who edits a survivor's spoken account for a published transcript, the documentary filmmaker who scores archival footage with music, the human rights lawyer who extracts from a client's testimony the elements most useful for a legal argument: all are doing what their medium requires. None of them can bear witness without also becoming, to some degree, a curator.

Granted, curation need not mean betrayal. The most ethically serious documentary practitioners — journalists like Ryszard Kapuściński, photographers like Sebastião Salgado, memoirists like Svetlana Alexievich — have developed forms of practice that hold the tension rather than resolving it: they make their interpretive choices visible, they attribute uncertainty to themselves rather than to the person they are depicting, they return to subjects, they allow contradiction to remain in the final account. Even so, no technique fully dissolves the asymmetry. The practitioner goes home; the subject stays. The practitioner's reputation may be made by the account; the subject must live with whatever image the account has given them in the public record.

The deepest problem is not that witnesses become curators — that is, as established, unavoidable. It is that they sometimes do so without acknowledging it: presenting a shaped account as transparent record, lending their own interpretive authority to a frame the witnessed person cannot contest because they do not know it exists, or because contesting it would require access to the same platforms and audiences that the witness already occupies. This is the particular ethical weight that attaches to documentary practice: it is not enough to have good intentions toward the person you are depicting. You must also hold, explicitly and without comfort, the knowledge that your account is always a partial act of possession — and that the more moving it is, the more thoroughly it has been curated, and the further it has travelled from the experience that made it possible.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Inversion with 'only when' to delay the critical threshold
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'Only when the witness begins to make choices about what to include does the act of recording become the act of composing' dùng đảo ngữ được kích hoạt bởi 'Only when'. Thay vì 'The act of recording only becomes the act of composing when…', cấu trúc đảo ngữ đặt điều kiện lên đầu và đảo trợ động từ ('does'), tạo ra cảm giác bước qua ngưỡng: người đọc phải đi qua điều kiện trước khi đến kết luận đáng lo ngại.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The opening 'Only when the witness begins to make choices about what to include does the act of recording become the act of composing' uses inversion triggered by 'Only when'. Rather than 'The act of recording only becomes the act of composing when…', the inversion fronts the condition and inverts the auxiliary ('does'), creating a threshold-crossing effect: the reader must pass through the condition before reaching the troubling conclusion.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence to isolate the precise moment of transition
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: Câu 'It is precisely the moment when the witness begins to care about the quality of their account — when the prose becomes elegant, when the angle is adjusted, when the detail is selected for its emotional resonance rather than its accuracy — that the transition from witnessing to curating occurs.' dùng cấu trúc tách 'It is X that Y'. Chuỗi mệnh đề 'when' bên trong ngoặc kép em-dash liệt kê các dấu hiệu cụ thể của sự chuyển đổi, làm cho luận điểm trừu tượng trở nên cụ thể và nhận ra được.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: The cleft 'It is precisely the moment when the witness begins to care about the quality of their account — when the prose becomes elegant, when the angle is adjusted, when the detail is selected for its emotional resonance rather than its accuracy — that the transition from witnessing to curating occurs.' uses 'It is X that Y'. The chain of 'when' clauses inside the em-dash enumerates specific signs of the transition, making the abstract argument concrete and recognisable.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chỉ khi nhân chứng bắt đầu đưa ra lựa chọn về những gì cần đưa vào, hành động ghi chép mới trở thành hành động sáng tác — và chính tại ngưỡng này, gần như vô hình với chính nhân chứng, đạo đức của tài liệu trở nên thực sự khó khăn. Một nhân chứng không lựa chọn không phải là nhân chứng mà là camera giám sát. Lựa chọn là không thể tránh khỏi. Nhưng lựa chọn cũng là sự khởi đầu của giám tuyển, và giám tuyển là sự khởi đầu của việc áp đặt ý nghĩa mà người được chứng kiến không đồng ý và có thể không nhận ra là của mình.

Sự căng thẳng rõ ràng nhất trong báo ảnh. Hãy xem xét các bức ảnh được chụp trong cuộc diệt chủng Rwanda năm 1994, nhiều bức bởi các nhà báo phương Tây. Những hình ảnh được lưu hành rộng rãi nhất — xác người trên đường, những người sống sót gầy guộc, hình học của tội ác hàng loạt được làm rõ để tiêu thụ quốc tế — được chọn không phải bởi những người được miêu tả mà bởi các biên tập viên ảnh ở London, Paris và New York. Bức ảnh nổi tiếng của Kevin Carter về con kền kền và đứa trẻ ở Sudan — chụp năm 1993, được trao giải Pulitzer — có lẽ là trường hợp rõ ràng nhất về logic giám tuyển bên trong xung lực ghi chép: hình ảnh được sáng tác để tạo ra một phản ứng, lịch sử riêng của đứa trẻ không liên quan đến phản ứng mà nó tạo ra, và sự nghiệp — và cái chết, và di sản — của nhiếp ảnh gia đã hấp thụ cuộc trò chuyện đạo đức đáng lẽ phải về đứa trẻ.

Chính xác là khoảnh khắc khi nhân chứng bắt đầu quan tâm đến chất lượng tài khoản của họ — khi văn xuôi trở nên thanh lịch, khi góc độ được điều chỉnh, khi chi tiết được chọn vì sự cộng hưởng cảm xúc hơn là độ chính xác — mà sự chuyển đổi từ làm chứng sang giám tuyển xảy ra. Đây không phải là thất bại của ý định; đó là hậu quả cấu trúc của thực tế rằng tài liệu đòi hỏi một phương tiện, và tất cả các phương tiện đều có tính thẩm mỹ.

Cứ cho là giám tuyển không nhất thiết có nghĩa là phản bội. Những người hành nghề tài liệu nghiêm túc về mặt đạo đức nhất — các nhà báo như Ryszard Kapuściński, nhiếp ảnh gia như Sebastião Salgado, tác giả hồi ký như Svetlana Alexievich — đã phát triển các hình thức thực hành giữ vững sự căng thẳng thay vì giải quyết nó: họ làm cho những lựa chọn diễn giải của mình trở nên rõ ràng, họ quy sự không chắc chắn cho bản thân thay vì cho người được miêu tả, họ cho phép mâu thuẫn tồn tại trong tài khoản cuối cùng. Dù vậy, không có kỹ thuật nào hoàn toàn giải thể sự bất đối xứng. Người hành nghề về nhà; đối tượng ở lại.

Vấn đề sâu sắc nhất không phải là các nhân chứng trở thành người giám tuyển — điều đó, như đã được thiết lập, là không thể tránh khỏi. Mà là họ đôi khi làm như vậy mà không thừa nhận: trình bày một tài khoản được định hình như hồ sơ minh bạch, cho vay quyền lực diễn giải của riêng họ cho một khung mà người được chứng kiến không thể tranh cãi vì họ không biết nó tồn tại, hoặc vì việc tranh cãi nó đòi hỏi quyền truy cập vào cùng các nền tảng mà nhân chứng đã chiếm giữ. Đây là trọng lượng đạo đức đặc biệt gắn với thực hành tài liệu: không đủ để có ý định tốt đối với người bạn đang miêu tả. Bạn cũng phải giữ, một cách rõ ràng và không thoải mái, kiến thức rằng tài khoản của bạn luôn là một hành động chiếm hữu một phần — và nó càng lay động lòng người, nó càng được giám tuyển kỹ lưỡng hơn, và nó càng đi xa khỏi trải nghiệm đã làm cho nó có thể.

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why is it impossible to be a pure witness without any curation?
options: Because witnesses always have hidden agendas
Because selection is unavoidable — a witness who does not select is a surveillance camera
Because the media always edits witness accounts
Because witnesses are too emotionally involved
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states: 'A witness who does not select is not a witness but a surveillance camera. Selection is unavoidable. Yet selection is also the beginning of curation.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the sentence about surveillance cameras.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the Kevin Carter photograph example illustrate?
options: That photojournalists are always unethical
The curatorialist logic inside the documentary impulse: the image was composed to produce a response, and the child's own history was irrelevant to that response
That the Pulitzer Prize should not reward war photography
That photographers should not travel to conflict zones
mcQuestions.explanation: Carter's photograph is described as 'the clearest case of the curatorialist logic inside the documentary impulse': the response it produced mattered more than the child's history.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the essay's explicit conclusion about what the photograph is a case of.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'only when' inversion?
options: Only when the witness begins to make choices the act of recording becomes composing.
Only when the witness begins to make choices about what to include does the act of recording become the act of composing.
The act of recording only when the witness chooses does become composing.
Only when does the witness begin to choose, the act becomes composing.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Only when X does Y' requires inversion: 'does the act… become' not 'the act… becomes'.
mcQuestions.hint: The auxiliary must invert after 'only when'.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay identifies the 'transition from witnessing to curating'. What triggers this transition?
options: When the witness decides to publish their account
When the witness begins to care about the quality and emotional resonance of their account rather than its accuracy
When the witness is paid for their work
When the account is translated into another language
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft sentence identifies 'precisely the moment when the witness begins to care about the quality of their account — when… the detail is selected for its emotional resonance rather than its accuracy'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the cleft sentence describing when the transition occurs.

options: The process of making suffering more bearable through art therapy
The transformation of someone's suffering into an aesthetically pleasing representation that serves audiences rather than the person who suffered
The study of beauty in difficult circumstances
Creating public art about social injustice
mcQuestions.explanation: Aestheticisation of suffering means rendering someone's pain in a way that prioritises aesthetic effect for an audience over the dignity of the person being represented.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'aesthetic' as relating to beauty and artistic form — applied to suffering.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is 'epistemic humility' as implied by the essay?
options: Admitting you don't know enough to have an opinion
The intellectual virtue of acknowledging the limits of your knowledge and perspective, especially when representing others
Being modest about your educational achievements
Refusing to publish your findings
mcQuestions.explanation: The ethical practitioners described in the essay demonstrate epistemic humility by 'making their interpretive choices visible' and 'attributing uncertainty to themselves rather than to the person they are depicting.'
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'epistemic' as relating to knowledge, and 'humility' as acknowledging limits.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's concession, and what does it concede to?
options: Concession: photojournalism is always harmful. Counter-claim: it is sometimes necessary.
Concession: curation need not mean betrayal. Counter-claim: but no technique dissolves the asymmetry between practitioner and subject.
Concession: witnesses have good intentions. Counter-claim: but intentions are irrelevant.
Concession: documentary practice is ethical. Counter-claim: but it rarely works.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay grants 'curation need not mean betrayal' and praises ethical practitioners, then counters with 'Even so, no technique fully dissolves the asymmetry.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'Granted… Even so…' paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'the practitioner goes home; the subject stays' mean?
options: Photojournalists travel more than their subjects
The asymmetry between witness and subject is permanent: the practitioner can leave, but the subject continues to live with the account made of their experience
Journalists should spend more time with the people they document
Subjects should be compensated financially
mcQuestions.explanation: This sentence encapsulates the structural asymmetry: the practitioner's relationship to the account is temporary; the subject's is permanent.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about who controls the account after it is made.

options: The legal right to publish a story
The power to determine how a story is told, including what is included, framed, and interpreted
A government body that regulates media
The authority given to narrators in literary fiction
mcQuestions.explanation: Narrative authority is the power to shape a story — the essay argues that documentary practitioners hold this authority over the people they depict.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'authority' as power, applied to how stories are constructed.

mcQuestions.prompt: The essay mentions Kapuściński, Salgado, and Alexievich. What do these practitioners have in common according to the essay?
options: They all work in war zones
They have developed practices that make their interpretive choices visible and hold the tension between witnessing and curating rather than concealing it
They are the most famous journalists of the twentieth century
They have all been criticised for ethical failures
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay praises them for practices that 'hold the tension rather than resolving it' — making choices visible, attributing uncertainty to themselves, allowing contradiction.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for what the essay says they do that distinguishes them.

options: Showing too much empathy in a professional setting
The error of believing you understand someone's experience so completely that you feel entitled to speak for them
Reaching out to help someone who doesn't want help
A medical condition related to excessive emotion
mcQuestions.explanation: Empathic overreach is the overconfident assumption that one's empathy gives one the right to represent another's experience — the witness believes they understand so well they can speak for the subject.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'overreach' as going too far, combined with 'empathy'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which correctly uses a cleft to identify the moment of transition?
options: The witness begins to care about quality precisely when the transition occurs.
When the witness begins to care about quality, then the transition occurs.
It is precisely the moment when the witness begins to care about the quality of their account that the transition from witnessing to curating occurs.
What occurs is the transition when witness begins to care precisely.
mcQuestions.explanation: Only option C uses the 'It is X that Y' cleft structure with 'precisely' to isolate the exact moment.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the 'It is… that…' structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's deepest concern about witnessing?
options: That witnesses are not paid enough for their work
That witnesses present shaped accounts as transparent records, lending their authority to a frame the witnessed person cannot contest
That most documentary photography is technically poor
That witnesses suffer from secondary trauma
mcQuestions.explanation: The final paragraph identifies the deepest problem: presenting a 'shaped account as transparent record, lending their own interpretive authority to a frame the witnessed person cannot contest.'
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the paragraph beginning 'The deepest problem is not…'

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the phrase 'your account is always a partial act of possession' mean?
options: The account is only partly accurate
Every account of another person's experience takes partial ownership of that experience, imposing the documenter's frame on it
You only own part of the copyright to your account
Accounts should always be partial — full accounts are impossible
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that documentation always involves a degree of 'possession' — taking the other person's experience and making it into one's own account, which serves one's own purposes to some extent.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'possession' as owning or appropriating, not just holding.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central argument?
options: Documentary practitioners should stop curating and simply record.
The tension between witnessing and curating is unavoidable but must be held consciously, not concealed — and the more moving an account, the more it has been curated.
Photojournalism is inherently unethical.
Witnesses should always obtain consent before publishing.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's through-line is that curation is unavoidable, ethical practice means holding the tension consciously and visibly, and the deepest failure is presenting curation as transparent witnessing.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the argument that accepts unavoidability while insisting on acknowledgement.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Have you ever been the subject of someone else's account — a story told about you, a photograph taken of you, a description shared with others — that felt like it captured something true but was also, in some way, not quite yours? What had the person who made the account selected, and what had they left out? What does this tell you about the ethics of representation?
